title: Common Tool Integration Mistakes
concept: No timeouts, raw exceptions returned, weak parameter validation, and over-privileged permissions.

exercises.prompt: Last lesson: why does giving an agent too many tools hurt its accuracy?

options.text: More tools means the agent gets confused about which one to pick
Tools cost extra money each time they load
Agents can only ever hold exactly one tool

exercises.explain: Too many tools crowd the agent's attention, so it struggles to choose the right one. Grouping or scoping toolsets keeps choices clear and accuracy high.

cards.title: When Good Tools Go Bad
cards.body: A tool can work perfectly in a demo and still break your agent in the real world. The usual culprits are sloppy setup choices, not the AI itself.

cards.title: The Four Classic Mistakes
cards.body: No timeouts (a tool hangs forever), raw exceptions (ugly errors leak straight back), weak validation (bad inputs slip through), and over-privileged permissions (a tool can do more than it should).

cards.title: Like a Car With No Safety Checks
cards.body: Imagine a car with no timer for the engine, no seatbelt for inputs, and keys that open every door in town. It might drive fine today, but one bad moment causes a crash.

cards.title: The Tool That Never Returns
cards.body: An agent calls a weather tool, but the weather site is down. With no timeout, the agent just waits... and waits... frozen forever. A 5-second timeout would let it recover and try something else.

cards.title: "The AI Will Handle Errors"
cards.body: Myth: a smart agent can clean up any messy error on its own. Truth: if a tool returns a raw crash message, the agent often gets confused. Give it clear, friendly error text instead.

cards.title: Sloppy Tool vs. Safe Tool
cards.body: Small setup choices decide whether a tool helps or hurts your agent.

left.text: No timeout, raw errors, any input allowed, full admin access

right.text: Timeouts set, clean error messages, inputs checked, least access needed

exercises.prompt: An agent's file tool can read, write, AND delete any file on the whole computer, but it only ever needs to read one folder. What mistake is this?
exercises.explain: The tool can do far more than its job requires. Giving it only read access to that one folder (least privilege) limits the damage if anything goes wrong.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Setting a timeout on a tool helps an agent recover when an external service hangs.
exercises.explain: A timeout stops the agent from waiting forever. When the tool takes too long, it fails gracefully and the agent can retry or choose another path.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank about checking inputs.
exercises.sentence: Weak parameter validation means bad inputs slip through instead of being checked before the tool runs.
exercises.explain: Parameter validation checks that inputs make sense before running. Weak validation lets bad data through, which can crash the tool or produce wrong results.

exercises.prompt: Which tool error message was written by an AI to be clear and agent-friendly?

options.text: The city name was not found. Please provide a valid city, like 'Paris' or 'Tokyo', and try again.
ERR_0x8F: unhandled trace dump; stack overflow module core

exercises.explain: The first message explains what went wrong in plain language and tells the agent how to fix it. The others are raw, technical crash dumps that confuse the agent.

exercises.prompt: Your agent's payment tool waits forever when the bank is slow, returns raw crash logs, accepts any text as an amount, and can move money from every account. Which fix set addresses ALL four mistakes at once?

options.text: Add a timeout, return clean error messages, validate the amount, and limit the tool to only the needed account
Give the tool more permissions so it never gets blocked
Remove all error messages so the agent never sees problems
Let the tool wait as long as it needs and trust every input

exercises.explain: Each fix maps to one classic mistake: timeouts stop hangs, clean errors replace raw crashes, validation blocks bad inputs, and least-privilege access limits reach. Together they turn a risky tool into a safe, reliable one your agent can trust.
